immediately drop into the toilet at the best a one month retention on auto ship deliver that newsletter as an extra benefit to my auto ship people boom retention back up to four months which is double industry standard it can you still trust me on this you still cannot replicate this the power of that with any digital format period okay so specific copy stuff so you critique a lot of stuff you do a lot of copywriting like me you see a lot of your subscriber stuff your mastermind group people's crossed your desk what's some of the worst dumbest stuff most ineffectual stuff that people keep doing that you see most what I see most is selling to logic selling to the logical brain feature benefit selling which you know it ain't bad better than nothing right but not taking the time to figure out what their market really wants not taking the time to really understand the market focusing on product features benefits where it's all about the market you know he you've said glop a couple times this weekend and that's like I mean that affectionately you'll of course we all do it's enough it's a term of affection that us copy writers use but but there's there's a lot to be said about that because the product really the important thing it's the market which and let's let's let me clarify a little bit of markets are just groups of people so it's actually we're talking about people we're talking about the person you're selling to so I see a lot of copy it's just all feature benefits it's does this it has this it's blue it has seven handles that you know when you quit exercising on it after two days you can hang your clothes on it so feature benefit not just not understanding the market enough and why people are buying this not buying it for any of the features they're not buying it for any of the benefits that you think they're buying it for even the benefits they say they're buying it for they're getting to the deeper benefits and and targeting those the unstated the unstated reasons why people want to buy stuff yeah so people focusing way too much on product and just not focusing on and understanding the market so that's what's what you do the most important of everything you just said is even the benefits they state so you know there's a direct sales axiom that buyers are liars and and that's why focus groups are flawed surveys are flawed interviews with customers are flawed is because in many many cases they will go to the same place that the journeyman copywriter went they will go to product feature benefit uses the logical functionality of the thing that they bought that's where they'll go default to just as most marketers will default to it and that's how they've justified and rationalized the purchase after the fact it's the context they think they want you want information about and so all that stuff it's not that you don't do it but it's it has to be looked at with great skepticism and caution when you survey a bunch of customers about either why they did what they did or what they want in a given product or service because they in most cases they don't know and they can't really enunciate why they have behaved in the way they have behaved and in some cases if they can they find it embarrassing and so they won't enunciate why they have behaved in the way they have behaved right so if you take that split test on that headline I showed you of I can't do it verbatim now but you're gonna lose X number of pounds or you're gonna lose more weight than any of your friends do in the next 90 days in the second headline correct vastly outperforms first for obvious reasons because of positional status even if Barbara wants to lose weight she really likes the idea losing more weight than Cindy and Susie particularly Susie always has a better car and a better dress and a better husband and a better boyfriend and a better and this is finally an opportunity to do and she's not going to enunciate that back to you in like an interview we're in a focus group unless it's very skillfully conducted she's never going to tell it to you ahead of time on a survey because it really hadn't occurred to her as why she would want to lose weight so the the truth in other words of why we do what we do and why we buy what we buy and why we want to own what we own and why we respond to one sales message or another is very hard to get at on a conscious intellectual conversational basis and and so I'm always and so back to your digital thing just as an example most audiences today in who are newsletter subscribers who are book buyers who are whatever large segments of that audience will tell you they want or would prefer digital and then their behavior after the fact will be the exact opposite of that and and of course but see they can't even describe to you how different parts of their brains are triggered by the act of opening a package versus the act of opening an email because they don't know that's happening to them so they can't tell us that it is happening right and that's that's a dangerous flaw a lot of people will over overweight gathered input from their own customers and and hurt themselves a lot by doing so what's the like the smartest thing you see people do who's who's the smartest pencil in your box maybe not by name but by description and what they're doing with their marketing so you got how many in so in nights of the you know whatever that you're roundtable so the smartest thing I see people doing well you know so here's a very very practical thing that the smartest guys in my Camelot do which I'm surprised more people don't do this I used to tell people to write at the sixth grade level and that's still a good goal because if you write at the sixth grade level well first of all that's the average that's the reading level of the average American so the guy who's got a PhD can understand it and then a guy like me who barely graduated from barberton high school can understand it and so I've been telling people that a lot and then I discovered something else the closer I got to the fourth grade reading level the higher my response went and Hemingway when you evaluate his stuff Hemingway wrote at the fourth grade reading level for all intents and purposes so so the best copy I'm seeing now for my nights is is sixth grade reading level or below watching the adverbs not overusing the adverbs using power verbs and Hemingway app Heming I think it's just Hemingway app.com will make you a lot of money if you run your copy through it it'll show you at what reading level you are it'll show which sentences are hard to understand which are really hard to understand and I have to fight people on this especially you know some of the health guys because they want to start they have all these great studies that they want to put in there and the Harvard this Harvard that and the closer we can get them to the fourth or sixth grade reading level the more money they make. So I used to have a Hemingway app which I used a lot and she grew up I mean but when Jennifer was eight nine ten I would have her read my copy aloud and watch what she stumbled over what she had difficulty with what she would ask about I just read it but I don't know what it means and I would go back and rewrite it all until she could do a smooth read with no hiccups and high comprehension of what she was reading. Your Hemingway app was way more expensive than mine. Yes you ain't jitter and it's now long ago stopped being functional but somehow the expense hasn't stopped I don't exactly know I don't know why that is but so I was just we were just talking about with with Kapoozie and Jeff before we started it's exactly what it's we will always tend to overestimate the intelligence the sophistication all of that of our audience because it's a it's flattering to us right everybody else is an idiot but not my people and so when you give them more credit you automatically give credit to yourself plus it seems to make sense right well my people are 80% college educated so you should deal with them on a different level than my investor's business daily readers are different than trailer park monthly readers we should deal with them on a different level and it's almost always wrong it's almost always wrong. I had that reinforced again you know sometimes I have to keep relearning these same lessons but had it reinforced recently just about two years ago I wrote a newspaper space ad yes people still buy from ads and newspapers much more than you probably think they do. Jeff Hawkins is Jeff still here or did Jeff stay for the first two days so the Dr. Hawkins the whole diabetes that's all based on that whole business has been driven by full-page newspaper ads that puts people in seminars free seminars and then drives them to an individual appointment to sell an eight thousand dollar diabetes box I mean and I just redid the newspaper ad and tweaked it a little bit but yeah some of this old media by the way if you can use it its cost has gone down because people are abandoning it right and so in many cases there's bargain opportunities in this stuff so anyway go ahead yeah yeah so I wrote an ad for a pain supplement and whatever it came in at like because I kept I was citing these studies and using all these fancy ingredients came in like the eighth grade reading level and the MER they measure the efficiency that an immediate media efficiency ratio it's just your return on ad spend so 1.0 MER is thousand dollar ad spend brings in a thousand dollars the MER came in at like point nine six yeah not bad. You live with that if there's a back end. You can live with that but I did something that I hadn't done in a really long time since the 90s I started listening to the calls and hearing how these people speak hearing their word choices and getting a mental picture of who this was reading this ad went back and redid it got as close to the fourth grade reading level as I can boom 3.0 MER just getting the reading level down for mom, Paul Kettle, and Barberton, Ohio, and the trailer park. Yeah and you know the longer you do what you do the further away you get from the customer or from the beginner right and you begin to think well everybody knows that right but no the new person doesn't know anymore than the new person knew a decade ago the new person is the new person the frightened person to our conversation they're just as frightened because they're coming into that office for the first time that's new to them right and the more we do it the longer you're in practice the long you know the more distanced we get from the sense of where they're at and you know yesterday absolutely did you feel like you have a better empathy for the people you're writing for after you heard those calls yeah I guess I mean I'm not a big empathy guy but I find that hard to believe Dan you know I have a lot of great attributes and that's relatively low on the list but but I had a better sense of where they were right because they were actually forcing a conversation beyond the script you know they really wanted to talk to somebody about their their history how many times they've tried to lose weight you know and they wanted to have a conversation about why they wanted to lose weight and the whole script was not set up for that you know and in call center so here's the interesting about call centers so that is another place that there's forced brevity so direct marketing companies that pay to use call centers for the most part they're paying by the minute so if you go hire an inbound call center to take your orders they're going to charge you often by the minute so now what everybody wants to do is have the call happen in the shortest number of minutes possible even with an inbound center so Weight Watchers was fielding five million calls a year and they wanted to do that with as few human beings as they could possibly do it with per shift thereby speed of call right and so they're monitoring that firing people who are taken too long given rewards at the end of the week to people who had the shortest average time on the call etc right so it's all about efficiency instead of effectiveness right there's a credit card commercial I think it's discover series of credit card commercials where the person calls in because they were late on their payment or something and the person on the other end of the phone in the discover center is like a clone of them and they're having a conversation with a clone of them right well that that's that's what people really want right I mean they want affinity they want you know they at least want empathy and if you can fake it that's okay right so they they're like all the pressure is to do these calls of the shortest period of time possible but the real issue is how many people who call in and get information about a meeting show up at the meeting that's like the first issue right and forcing unwanted data monitoring the longer calls higher show up right gee what a surprise right and so this script was all about moving Martha has quickly as possible to here's two locations there's a Tuesday night and a Thursday afternoon which one do you want to go to well how about if we let Martha tell her story a little right we don't really have to change what you say we just got to allow three minutes for Martha to tell her damn story about why she's trying to lose weight and say that's a really good reason to want to lose weight Martha now let me tell you where the two meetings are you know and makes a difference so so when you critique cops so people have asked about the copywriting process I'm not I'm gonna ask about your either self critique or critique of others how do you approach looking at somebody's copy and determining good bad ugly and being helpful the first thing I look at is you know we used to say you got about eight seconds to get somebody's attention I think that's changed and I blame the internet and smartphones it's probably you know maybe a couple seconds at the most so if you can't grab attention immediately I there's what sense does it make for anybody to read the rest of your car I don't care if you resurrected you know Jean Schwartz and Gary Halbert and they wrote the rest of the body copy nobody's gonna read it so I mean that's numero uno for me is does this stop me dead in my tracks and make me want to read on and it's harder to do that than ever in my most humble but accurate opinion these days so so so that's number one and actually I could just repeat that for there's a test for that it's not a new test there's a test for this so here's a take home if you don't already have it the test is you take no more than the top one third of the real estate of your ad or your letter or your website or your whatever down to maybe just the headline but you give yourself no more than the top third of the real estate make that the ad from which you attach the call to action so that's all you got is the headline or the pre-head the headline in the subhead and now call 1-800-WATCHES or go to www.watsis and if you ran that ad in the newspaper or in a magazine that your audience reads would people respond to just that and if they won't you're in trouble because the way they won't respond to it is in actual life is they won't go any further than it so to your point all of the rest of the brilliance is largely wasted right so that's a test it's an old test but it is still it's probably more relevant today to your point than than it has ever been what else you look at the big idea and is certain often wrong isn't it often wrong especially in health and financial I mean this these are markets that have heard and seen it all so your big idea better be pretty damn big and and pretty unique you know when everybody was talking about you know here's how to get a 10-banger with this brand new tech company you know here's how to you know MIT graduates amazing new system for doubling the value of your portfolio and yada yada number of weeks months whatever all of a sudden Stansbury says the end of America and you know just does the opposite it talks about how you know we're gonna have this big disaster the biker zombie apocalypse is coming and this is why you're gonna you know you're you're gonna lose all your money and stuff like that it was at the time I mean you know now everybody's ripped it off so that that market is blind to that kind of stuff almost almost but at the time that was a unique idea different than what everybody else is saying so if you're saying the same thing as everybody else well why should I read the rest of your piece I've seen it a million times before or worse and I bought this before and it didn't work so big idea is is you know is is one of the first things I look at I
